content.assessment: A presumption-against-closure duty addresses the procedural gap in Hungary's centralized system but does not alter the underlying demographic trajectory. The cohort collapse (22% birth decline 2021-2025, TFR 1.31) and the existence of a zero-enrollment cliff in the smallest settlements mean that for a meaningful subset of villages, 'alternatives assessment' will document genuine non-viability rather than prevent closure. The lens value of this proposal lies in ensuring closures track actual demographic contraction rather than administrative convenience, and in creating a documented counterfactual record where none currently exists.
opportunities: Could be calibrated using demographic momentum data (14.4% youth cohort share) to set realistic planning horizons rather than reactive, single-year closure decisions
Alternatives assessment could formally incorporate spatial clustering of aging municipalities (Vas/Zala) to plan region-specific responses ahead of crisis points
strengths: Creates a documented decision trail distinguishing genuinely unviable settlements (zero-cohort cliff) from administratively convenient closures
Procedural safeguard can incorporate age-structure and cohort-trajectory data into mandatory alternatives assessment
threats: Negative demographic momentum continues independent of procedural reform, meaning even a fully functioning presumption-against-closure regime cannot indefinitely sustain cohorts approaching zero
Delay introduced by mandatory assessment could outpace the speed of cohort collapse in the fastest-declining municipalities
weaknesses: Does not address the structural cause of closure pressure: TFR collapse to 1.31 and 22% four-year birth decline that shrink the underlying pupil pool regardless of procedure
Provides no mechanism for settlements that have already crossed the zero-enrollment threshold, such as villages with under-18 shares below 10 percent
